blocks.text: Bear one another's burdens, and in this way you will fulfill the law of Christ.
2 : Here the Gentiles seem to fulfill the law in some way; see also 5.14; 1 Cor 9.21; Rom 8.2.
For if those who are nothing think they are something, they deceive themselves.
All must test their own work; then that work, rather than their neighbor's work, will become a cause for pride.
For all must carry their own loads.
3–5 : Paul again warns that self-directed behavior will lead to a bad outcome at the impending judgment.
Those who are taught the word must share in all good things with their teacher.
Do not be deceived; God is not mocked, for you reap whatever you sow.
If you sow to your own flesh, you will reap corruption from the flesh; but if you sow to the Spirit, you will reap eternal life from the Spirit.
So let us not grow weary in doing what is right, for we will reap at harvest time, if we do not give up.
7–9 : Paul again warns about the ways of the versus “pneuma” ( ), here emphasizing the possible reward of (see also Rom 8.5–8).
9 : , see 2 Thess 3.13; in 2 Cor 4.1,16; Eph 3.13, the same verb is translated as “not lose heart.”
So then, whenever we have an opportunity, let us work for the good of all, and especially for those of the family of faith.
5.13–6.10: Ethics among the Christ-elect. In a shift of topic and tone, Paul presents a series of moral teachings that center on mutuality and solidarity.
6.1–10: Paul exhorts the Galatians about ethical conduct.
10 : , lit., “members of the household of faithfulness.”
See what large letters I make when I am writing in my own hand!
11 : , Paul had dictated this letter to a secretary but writes the conclusion in his ; see 1 Cor 16.21; 2 Thess 3.17.
It is those who want to make a good showing in the flesh that try to compel you to be circumcised—only that they may not be persecuted for the cross of Christ.
Even the circumcised do not themselves obey the law, but they want you to be circumcised so that they may boast about your flesh.
May I never boast of anything except the cross of our Lord Jesus Christ, by which the world has been crucified to me, and I to the world.

12–14 : Paul paints an antagonistic picture of his rivals as boastful, as he does elsewhere (see Phil 3.2–4; Rom 2.17–24; 3.27; 4.2; 1 Cor 4.7; 2 Cor 11.12–13,18). He charges that their interest in circumcising the Gentiles stems from self-interested motives: and to avoid persecution. They are also portrayed as hypocritical, as they do not obey the law themselves. Paul sums up his own teachings as in ; positive use of boasting language also appears in Rom 5.2–3,11; 1 Cor 1.29–31; 2.2
For neither circumcision nor uncircumcision is anything; but a new creation is everything!
15 : See 5.6. , the new mode of being that is made possible by the “pneuma” (“Spirit”), given to those who are “in Christ”; cf. 2 Cor 5.17; Rom 8.19–23.
As for those who will follow this rule—peace be upon them, and mercy, and upon the Israel of God.

16 : is the teaching that Paul has advocated throughout the letter. , a phrase unique to this verse, equivalent to the “true Israel,” i.e., those who follow Paul's and not the teachings of his alleged rivals and opponents.
From now on, let no one make trouble for me; for I carry the marks of Jesus branded on my body.

17 : , perhaps the scars from injuries inflicted on Paul during his work as an apostle (2 Cor 6.4–5; 11.23–25). Paul may also be suggesting that his scars identify him as belonging to Christ like the brand marks on a slave.
May the grace of our Lord Jesus Christ be with your spirit, brothers and sisters. Amen.
6.11–18: Final warning about the dangers of rival teachers. A return to the polemical tone characteristic of the letter.

16–18 : A two-part blessing. Verse 16 has the form of a traditional Jewish blessing and is conditional, bestowed on those who follow Paul's teaching; it is the counterpart to the curse in 1.8. The address in v. 18 does not appear in the final blessing of Paul's other letters and expresses the hope that the former warm relationship between the apostle and the community will be restored.
18 : Cf. Phil 4.23; Philem 25.
